perturbation.original_text: Ameren Illinois disclose to Recipient, customer information protected from public disclosure pursuant to 815 ILCS 505/2HH (“Confidential Information”) for use by the Recipient and its officers, directors, agents, employees and contractors (collectively, the “Representatives”), on a need-to-know basis for the purpose of facilitating electric supply aggregation pursuant to 20 ILCS 3855/1-92.
perturbation.changed_text: Ameren Illinois may disclose to Recipient, customer information regardless of its protection from public disclosure, including but not limited to information protected pursuant to 815 ILCS 505/2HH (“Confidential Information”) for use by the Recipient and its officers, directors, agents, employees and contractors (collectively, the “Representatives”), without restriction on its use for the purpose of facilitating electric supply aggregation pursuant to 20 ILCS 3855/1-92.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the protection of customer information and allows for unrestricted use, contradicting the statutory intent of 815 ILCS 505/2HH.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Illinois Consumer Fraud and Deceptive Business Practices Act 815 ILCS 505/2HH
perturbation.law_explanation: 815 ILCS 505/2HH protects customer information from public disclosure. The modified text allows Ameren Illinois to disclose protected information regardless of this law, effectively nullifying the consumer protection afforded by the statute. The original language protects customer information from public disclosure, but the new language allows for that information to be disclosed regardless of its protection, which violates the intent of the statute.

perturbation.justification: The original text protects customer information from public disclosure pursuant to 815 ILCS 505/2HH. The changed text explicitly allows Ameren Illinois to disclose this protected information regardless of its protection, effectively nullifying the consumer protection intended by the statute. The provided law snippet 1 references the Illinois Compiled Statutes, which include 815 ILCS 505/2HH. The modification directly contradicts the statute's intent to protect customer information.

perturbation.original_text: Under this Agreement, all information disclosed by the Ameren Illinois whether provided in oral, written, visual, electronic or other form is presumed to be Confidential Information unless it falls within one of the exclusions of Section 3. Confidential Information includes non-public customer specific information.
perturbation.changed_text: Under this Agreement, only information explicitly designated as 'Confidential' in writing by Ameren Illinois is considered Confidential Information. Confidential Information excludes non-public customer specific information.
perturbation.explanation: This change significantly narrows the definition of 'Confidential Information', excluding non-public customer information, contradicting the original broad definition and potentially violating consumer privacy laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Illinois Personal Information Protection Act (PIPA), 815 ILCS 530
perturbation.law_explanation: The Illinois Personal Information Protection Act (PIPA) aims to protect the personal information of Illinois residents. By excluding non-public customer specific information from the definition of 'Confidential Information,' the modified text weakens the protection of such information, potentially conflicting with PIPA's objectives. The statute considers this as personal information, but the new text excludes it.

perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a legal contradiction by narrowing the definition of 'Confidential Information' and excluding non-public customer specific information, which the law explanation states is protected under the Illinois Personal Information Protection Act (PIPA). The original text explicitly included non-public customer information as Confidential Information. By excluding it, the change weakens protections and potentially violates PIPA's objectives. The scraped law snippet 1 shows that the Illinois Compiled Statutes (ILCS) are the official source of Illinois law, implying that PIPA's requirements for protecting personal information must be adhered to, thus creating a contradiction if customer specific information is removed from the definition of 'Confidential Information'.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient shall maintain the confidentiality of any Confidential I nformation and shall not disclose such Confidential Information, in whole or in part, to any person other than its Representatives who need to know such Confidential Information. Representatives shall be informed by the Recipient of the confidential nature of the Confidential Information and shall be directed by the Recipient to treat the Confidential Information confidentially, except with the prior written consent of Ameren Illinois or as otherwise permitted hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient shall endeavor to maintain the confidentiality of any Confidential Information but may disclose such Confidential Information, in whole or in part, to any person with a perceived need to know such Confidential Information. Representatives are not required to be informed of the confidential nature of the Confidential Information and are not directed by the Recipient to treat the Confidential Information confidentially.
perturbation.explanation: This weakens the obligation to maintain confidentiality and removes the requirement to inform Representatives, increasing the risk of unauthorized disclosure.
perturbation.law_explanation: While this agreement isn't explicitly about trade secrets, the weakening of confidentiality provisions undermines protections similar to those afforded by the Illinois Trade Secrets Act. By not requiring representatives to be informed or treat information confidentially, the agreement fails to take reasonable measures to protect potentially confidential information, as required to maintain trade secret status. In short, it weakens protections.

perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly weakens the obligation to maintain confidentiality. The original text mandates maintaining confidentiality and informing representatives of the confidential nature, directing them to treat the information as such. The changed text weakens this by stating the recipient shall 'endeavor' to maintain confidentiality and removes the requirement to inform representatives, increasing the risk of unauthorized disclosure. The law explanation suggests this could undermine protections similar to those afforded by the Illinois Trade Secrets Act by failing to take reasonable measures to protect confidential information. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is primarily procedural, it highlights the importance of accurate and up-to-date legal information, reinforcing the need for contracts to align with legal standards for protecting confidential information. Scraped Law Snippet 2 is not applicable, but Snippet 1 and the Law Explanation, along with the nature of the modification, support the determination of a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Ameren Illinois shall not be responsible for damages awarded due to the improper disclosure of consumer information that is adjudicated to have been caused by the Recipient’s negligence or intentional conduct.
perturbation.changed_text: Ameren Illinois shall only be responsible for damages awarded due to the improper disclosure of consumer information that is adjudicated to have been caused solely by Ameren Illinois' gross negligence, superseding any negligence by the recipient.
perturbation.explanation: This significantly shifts liability to Ameren Illinois even if the Recipient is negligent, contrary to general tort principles.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Illinois Civil Justice Reform Amendments of 1995, 735 ILCS 5/2-1116
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification attempts to eliminate the responsibility of Recipient by creating a higher bar for liability for Ameren, even when the Recipient is negligent. 735 ILCS 5/2-1116 deals with joint and several liability and contribution among tortfeasors. This attempted shift of sole responsibility may contradict principles of comparative fault and contribution established in Illinois law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Illinois General Assembly - Illinois Compiled Statutes Home Legislation & Laws Senate House My Legislation Site Map Bills & Resolutions Compiled Statutes Public Acts Legislative Reports IL Constitution Legislative Guide Legislative Glossary Search By Number (example: HB0001) Search Tips Search By Keyword Search Tips Advanced Search Illinois Compiled Statutes ILCS Listing Public Acts Search Guide Disclaimer Information maintained by the Legislative Reference Bureau Updating the database of the Illinois Compiled Statutes (ILCS) is an ongoing process. Recent laws may not yet be included in the ILCS database, but they are found on this site as Public Acts soon after they become law. For information concerning the relationship between statutes and Public Acts, refer to the Guide . Because the statute database is maintained primarily for legislative drafting purposes, statutory changes are sometimes included in the statute database before they take effect. If the source note at the end of a Section of the statutes includes a Public Act that has not yet taken effect, the version of the law that is currently in effect may have already been removed from the database and you should refer to that Public Act to see the changes made to the current law. 735 ILCS 5/2-1116 (735 ILCS 5/2-1116) (from Ch. 110, par. 2-1116) Sec. 2-1116. Limitation on recovery in tort actions. In all actions on account of bodily injury or death or physical damage to property, based on negligence, or product liability based on strict tort liability, the plaintiff shall be barred from recovering damages if the trier of fact finds that the contributory fault on the part of the plaintiff is more than 50% of the proximate cause of the injury or damage for which recovery is sought. The plaintiff shall not be barred from recovering damages if the trier of fact finds that the contributory fault on the part of the plaintiff is not more than 50% of the proximate cause of the injury or damage for which recovery is sought, but any damages allowed shall be diminished in the proportion to the amount of fault attributable to the plaintiff. No contributory fault may be attributed to a plaintiff bringing an action for damages for personal injury based on childhood sexual abuse as defined in Section 13-202.2. (Source: P.A. 103-1053, eff. 12-20-24.) perturbation.justification: The modified text shifts liability to Ameren Illinois, even if the Recipient is negligent, by stating Ameren Illinois shall only be responsible for damages awarded due to the improper disclosure of consumer information that is adjudicated to have been caused solely by Ameren Illinois' gross negligence, superseding any negligence by the recipient. This contradicts the principle of comparative fault and contribution, where multiple parties can be held liable based on their degree of fault. Snippet 1, 735 ILCS 5/2-1116, discusses contributory fault and diminishing damages based on the amount of fault attributable to the plaintiff. The modified text attempts to eliminate the Recipient's responsibility, which is not in line with comparative fault as addressed in 735 ILCS 5/2-1116.
